strongest_counterargument: The market's epidemiological prominence could result from ascertainment bias if it was the first location subjected to intensive testing after an infected person visited it, rather than being the true origin. A lab-associated infected individual visiting the market could seed a detectable cluster there while earlier cases went undetected elsewhere, particularly since early COVID cases with mild symptoms would be clinically indistinguishable from common respiratory illnesses without targeted surveillance.
what_would_refute_this_counterargument: Comprehensive retrospective serological surveys of hospital samples from November-December 2019 across Wuhan showing no COVID antibodies outside the market area, or genetic diversity data demonstrating the market cluster represents the root of the phylogenetic tree rather than a single introduction event. Definitive evidence of infected animals at the market with viral sequences ancestral to human cases would also be dispositive.
revised_confidence_rationale: The attestors are debate judges and participants analyzing the same underlying epidemiological data rather than independent empirical investigations. The fundamental challenge—distinguishing 'place of origin' from 'place of discovery'—remains unresolved without pre-outbreak baseline surveillance that didn't exist.
the_thing_nobody_is_saying: If a lab leak occurred, the market cluster would still be expected under most plausible transmission scenarios given Wuhan's size—the virus would spread until it hit a superspreader location, which markets are known to be. The real question isn't whether the market was involved, but whether we can distinguish a market origin from a market amplification event, and our surveillance methodology was too coarse-grained at the outbreak's start to make this distinction reliably.
